identity.title: Transportation Equipment and Supplies (except Motor Vehicle) Merchant Wholesalers

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market merchant wholesalers supplying recurring aftermarket parts, components, consumables, spares, exchange units, and related sourcing, inventory, documentation, and fulfillment services for aircraft, marine vessels, rail equipment, and other non-motor-vehicle transportation assets.
lens.excluded_activities: Whole-aircraft, vessel, or rolling-stock brokers dominated by episodic asset sales; manufacturers; repair stations whose economics are primarily labor service; captive OEM or carrier units; motor-vehicle parts distributors; financing vehicles; and firms lacking transferable operations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Product and exchange gross margin from repeat parts procurement by airlines, MROs, operators, government agencies, lessors, and transportation fleets, often supplemented by AOG response, pooling, consignment, inventory management, documentation, sourcing, repair coordination, and logistics fees.

a.bridge: BLS staffing evidence covers broader durable-goods wholesalers, while observed software capabilities focus on aviation RFQ and parts workflows rather than wage-weighted tasks across all 423860 transport modes. I map sales, procurement, customer service, office, inventory, pricing, and document handling to AI-exposed tasks, while assigning low substitution to inspection, warehousing, logistics, expert airworthiness judgment, relationship selling, and AOG accountability. Aviation's documentation burden may create more automatable clerical work but also more mandatory human verification, making net direction uncertain.
a.rationale: AI can extract emailed RFQs, search inventory and alternates, suggest prices, draft and send routine quotes, forecast parts demand, reconcile documents, and prioritize sourcing. However, high-value and safety-critical parts require provenance review, receiving inspection, serial and shelf-life checks, export and customer compliance, physical handling, and accountable decisions that constrain substitution.
a.change_evidence: A 423860 wage-weighted occupational study plus time-and-motion data from aviation, marine, and rail distributors measuring RFQ intake, quoting, sourcing, trace-document review, inspection, warehouse, logistics, AOG, sales, and finance tasks.
caveats: Aviation-specific tools may not generalize to marine and rail distributors.
Software capability does not establish current utilization or realized labor removal.
Safety and export controls can require human review even where documents are machine-readable.

rho.bridge: General distributor surveys report AI adoption and impact, while aviation vendors show technically feasible RFQ automation; neither measures the five-year implemented share for 423860 LMM firms. I discount adoption intentions for fragmented inventory systems, inconsistent part master data, cybersecurity, customer qualification, traceability, safety governance, export controls, and exception-heavy AOG work. Technology-oriented survey and vendor populations likely bias implementation upward.
rho.rationale: Distributor adoption is advancing, but full operational impact remains uncommon. Aviation-specific systems already automate RFQ extraction, pricing rules, and inventory search, suggesting near-term implementability for structured lanes. The compliance cost of false matches and the long tail of irregular parts keep human-in-the-loop workflows important.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal panel of 423860 firms reporting the share of RFQs, quotes, purchase orders, trace packages, and inventory decisions processed with AI, human exception and error rates, audit findings, implementation cost, and verified labor savings.
caveats: Adoption intent is not scaled implementation.
Sponsored tools describe best-case workflows.
Regulated customers may limit autonomous sending or approval even when extraction is accurate.

e.rationale: The code includes aircraft parts, marine and railroad equipment, commercial vessels, golf carts, and other transportation goods. Repeat aftermarket parts, spares, and inventory/sourcing programs fit the lens; episodic whole-equipment brokers and project-only suppliers do not. The Aviation Suppliers Association notes that the code is a useful but imperfect proxy for aircraft parts distribution, implying a substantial aftermarket cohort, but no source gives the LMM firm share meeting recurring-service and transferability requirements.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level classification of all 423860 LMM companies by revenue from aftermarket parts and repeat supply versus whole-equipment sales, plus customer concentration, supplier authorization, owner dependence, and recurring inventory-service contracts.
caveats: The estimate spans very different aviation, marine, rail, military, golf-cart, and other niches.
Some aircraft parts distributors classify outside 423860, while non-aviation firms inflate the code as an aviation proxy.
The injected LMM firm count is itself estimated.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures planned sale or transfer among all U.S. employer owners, not completed qualifying control transfers of eligible 423860 firms. I anchor near its 22% five-year figure, with observed aerospace-aftermarket transactions supporting buyer demand, then discount failed sales, non-control recapitalizations, closures, and targets outside the recurring-parts lens. Regulatory qualifications and supplier/customer approvals may depress completion, while strategic scarcity may raise it.
s5.rationale: Aging owners create succession supply, and recent aerospace aftermarket acquisitions demonstrate active strategic and private-equity interest. Transferability depends on qualified-vendor status, traceability systems, supplier rights, inventory quality, customer concentration, and whether technical and AOG relationships survive the owner.
s5.change_evidence: Closed 423860 control deals from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, SBA change-of-ownership lending, aviation brokers, and strategic acquirer disclosures, matched to annual eligible-firm counts and excluding recapitalizations and asset-only inventory sales.
caveats: Cross-industry owner intentions do not equal closed industry deals.
Publicly disclosed aerospace transactions skew larger than the LMM band.
Inventory purchases and repair-station deals may not transfer a qualifying distributor operation.

q.bridge: Aviation supply-chain evidence documents scarcity, AOG costs, OEM aftermarket power, and demand for better data rather than the discounted retained share of AI benefit at independent 423860 firms. I infer greater capture than commodity distribution because availability, qualification, documentation, and speed are valuable, while sophisticated buyers, auctions/marketplaces, OEM control, pooling, and contract repricing still share savings. Aviation conditions could overstate retention for marine, rail, and standardized supplies.
q.rationale: Automation can improve quote speed, sourcing reach, inventory turns, document quality, and AOG responsiveness. When aircraft availability is constrained, verified in-stock material has meaningful value and price is not the sole criterion. Yet OEMs capture substantial aftermarket economics, airlines and MROs use marketplaces and pooling, and repeat contracts can reprice, limiting permanent retention.
q.change_evidence: Deal- and line-level gross-margin, win-rate, response-time, inventory-turn, and customer-renewal data before and after AI deployment, split by AOG versus routine orders, sole-source versus competitive parts, OEM/USM/PMA, contract type, and transport mode.
caveats: No source directly measures five-year benefit retention.
AOG and hard-to-find parts have very different economics from routine consumables.
OEM authorization and lease-return rules can override distributor execution quality.

d5.bridge: FAA traffic and fleet forecasts plus IATA supply-chain evidence proxy aviation aftermarket activity, not constant-quality demand for the entire 423860 service basket. I translate rising flight activity, aging fleets, delayed deliveries, and spares needs into above-current five-year demand, then widen for airline cyclicality, normalization of bottlenecks, fleet retirements, and non-aviation segments. Aviation dominance in the evidence could overstate growth if rail or marine demand lags.
d5.rationale: FAA projects U.S. carrier revenue passenger miles rising 2.6% annually from 2025 to 2046 and domestic passengers 2.4% annually, while IATA reports an average fleet age of 15.1 years, a 17,000-aircraft backlog, and normalization of supply-demand mismatch unlikely before 2031-2034. Older active aircraft and constrained new deliveries support parts and maintenance demand through the five-year horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price 423860 product-line sales by aircraft, marine, and rail category; fleet utilization and retirement schedules; shop-visit forecasts; removals and replacement rates; new-aircraft deliveries; and parts pricing/quantity decompositions through 2031.
caveats: Passenger traffic does not map one-for-one to parts quantities.
Current shortages inflate inventory and substitution behavior that may normalize.
Defense, rail, marine, space, and general aviation can diverge from commercial airline trends.

o.bridge: FAA traceability guidance and IATA's description of accredited inventory, maintenance, pooling, and supply-chain functions establish continuing accountable work but do not directly measure the year-five operator-required quantity share. I assign a high share because physical custody, provenance, inspection, certification, logistics, credit, and AOG response remain necessary, while marketplaces, OEM direct sales, airline pooling, and customer self-sourcing can remove an independent intermediary. Aviation regulation may overstate operator necessity in less regulated transport niches.
o.rationale: Software can match, price, and route a request, but it cannot eliminate the need for an accountable entity to own or source the physical part, verify provenance and condition, manage documentation, ship it, and stand behind delivery. FAA guidance calls for traceability to approved sources and receiving inspection. Digital marketplaces may change who performs the role, but only a minority of the physical service basket becomes pure self-service.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement and invoice data tracking shares sourced direct from OEMs, through marketplaces or pools, and through independent distributors, alongside audits of which party retains legal, inspection, documentation, inventory, and delivery accountability.
caveats: Operator-required does not guarantee the operator remains an independent LMM distributor.
The estimate is highest for regulated aircraft parts and lower for standardized marine or rail supplies.
OEM vertical integration can preserve the function while eliminating the target firm type.

narrative.executive_view: Recurring transportation aftermarket distribution has a real AI productivity surface in RFQ intake, sourcing, pricing, inventory, and documentation, but its durable value rests on physical availability, provenance, qualification, and urgent response. Aviation evidence is strongest and suggests favorable five-year parts demand; the broad code also contains marine, rail, whole-equipment, and other niches, so a target must prove repeat aftermarket economics rather than merely carry the NAICS label.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can parse large emailed RFQs, map part numbers and alternates, search internal and marketplace inventory, recommend pricing, forecast spares, draft quotes, and organize trace documents. These tools reduce repetitive sales, procurement, and administrative work. Inspection, airworthiness and export judgment, supplier qualification, AOG escalation, physical logistics, and high-stakes customer commitments remain human-accountable and create a hard ceiling on autonomous implementation.
narrative.value_capture: Fast, accurate responses and better inventory decisions can create both cost and commercial value, especially for scarce and AOG parts. Qualification, in-stock availability, and clean documentation reduce pure price comparability. Capture is constrained by airline and MRO procurement sophistication, marketplaces, pooling, contract renewal, OEM aftermarket control, and the risk that improved data simply intensifies price competition.
narrative.firm_availability: Owner demographics and active aerospace aftermarket transactions indicate continuing control opportunities. A firm is transferable when approved-supplier status, traceability, inventory records, vendor access, customer relationships, and AOG processes are institutionalized. It is fragile when value resides in the owner's contacts or undocumented inventory knowledge. The qualifying share of the broad NAICS population remains uncertain.
narrative.demand_durability: Commercial aviation traffic is forecast to grow, while delayed deliveries and aging fleets sustain maintenance and spares demand through the horizon. Physical parts and accountability cannot be replaced by software. The main substitution risk is channel rather than end demand: OEM direct programs, digital marketplaces, pooling, and customer self-sourcing can move volume away from independent distributors even as total parts demand grows.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The evidence base is aviation-heavy and may not represent marine or rail distributors. Inventory obsolescence, counterfeit or unapproved parts, documentation failures, export controls, supplier concentration, OEM restrictions, lease-return rules, customer concentration, working capital, cyclicality, and AOG liability can dominate labor savings. A low-quality parts master or poor trace archive can also make AI unsafe and expensive to deploy.
narrative.principal_driver: High-volume RFQ, sourcing, pricing, and document workflows can be automated while scarce physical parts and traceability preserve operator value.
narrative.principal_weakness: The code is broad, and OEM control plus compliance-heavy exceptions make both eligibility and realized automation highly target-specific.

sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024
sources.supports: For the selected durable-goods wholesale grouping, leading occupations include 229,190 nontechnical wholesale/manufacturing sales representatives, 146,830 hand material movers, 77,370 general and operations managers, 62,040 stockers/order fillers, 42,160 customer service representatives, and 38,620 shipping/receiving/inventory clerks.

sources.title: AIquote aviation parts quotation platform
sources.supports: Describes automatic extraction of aviation RFQs from emails, pricing and inventory rules, automatic sending of qualifying quotes, and human dashboard review of exceptions, demonstrating exposure in a core industry workflow.

sources.title: FAA Advisory Circular 21-29D: Detecting and Reporting Suspected Unapproved Parts
sources.supports: Calls for traceability to FAA-approved sources and receiving inspection that verifies packaging, part and serial numbers, tampering, shelf or life limits, and supporting documents; identifies FAA Form 8130-3 and other approved records.

sources.title: IATA Aviation Supply Chain
sources.supports: Documents airline use of spares inventory, AOG desks, exchanges, loans, pooling, accredited suppliers, forecasting, and maintenance data; reports $1.4 billion in extra 2025 spares-inventory cost and describes OEM aftermarket dominance.

sources.title: Practical AI for Industrial Distribution
sources.supports: A 2025 distributor survey reported 29% AI use, about 10% positive business impact, more than 43% expected adoption within two years, and 50% ERP use; the article identifies fragmented data and integration as barriers.

sources.title: AI in distribution: driving digital transformation today
sources.supports: Reports that 90% of distributors had AI initiatives but only 11% had fully adopted, while 25% of adopters realized expected cost savings; logistics and inventory ranked as the leading AI-impact areas.

sources.title: How Big is the U.S. Aircraft Parts Distribution Industry?
sources.supports: Explains that 423860 includes commercial vessels, golf carts, railroad cars, and military vehicles as well as aircraft parts, and argues the code is an imperfect but useful proxy for aircraft parts distribution.

sources.title: Census Bureau profile: NAICS 423860
sources.supports: Identifies the national industry and reports 2,375 employer establishments, establishing the broad population to which the narrower recurring-aftermarket lens is applied.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 52.3% of U.S. employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years.

sources.title: MRO Memo: Where Is Aftermarket M&A Headed Next?
sources.supports: Reports that private-equity-led deals represented almost one-fifth of MRO-sector M&A activity in the first half of 2025 and notes buyer preference for predictable recurring repair volumes and scalable component businesses.

sources.title: Alliance Air Parts merger with RIM Enterprises
sources.supports: Documents a June 12, 2025 merger involving an Oklahoma City aircraft de-manufacturer and supplier to the business-aviation aftermarket, providing direct evidence of control activity in the parts ecosystem.

sources.title: Aerospace Supply Chain Bottlenecks Continue to Constrain Airlines
sources.supports: Reports a delivery shortfall of at least 5,300 aircraft, backlog above 17,000, average fleet age of 15.1 years, and supply-demand normalization unlikely before 2031-2034; also reports $3.1 billion in added maintenance cost and $1.4 billion in surplus inventory holding cost.

sources.title: Acquisition of FAA certified parts for consumable items
sources.supports: Requires specified dealer/distributor accreditation or traceability documentation for government acquisition of FAA-certified consumable parts, illustrating continuing qualification and documentation accountability.

sources.title: FAA Aerospace Forecast Fiscal Years 2026-2046: Forecast Overview
sources.supports: Forecasts U.S. carrier domestic passenger growth of 2.4% annually, system revenue passenger miles growth of 2.6% annually from 2025-2046, and turbine general-aviation fleet growth of 2.1% annually.
